passages.explanation: John the Baptiser appears in the wilderness of Judea, preaching: repent, for the Kingdom of Heaven is at hand. Matthew identifies him as the one Isaiah spoke of — a voice crying in the wilderness, make ready the way of the Lord. John wears camel's hair with a leather belt and eats locusts and wild honey — deliberately echoing Elijah. People from Jerusalem, all Judea, and the Jordan region come out to him, confessing their sins and being baptised. John's whole existence is a signpost. He doesn't build a movement around himself; he clears the road for someone else.

When John sees many Pharisees and Sadducees coming for baptism, he doesn't welcome them — he confronts them. You offspring of vipers, who warned you to flee from the coming wrath? Produce fruit worthy of repentance. Don't think you can say to yourselves, we have Abraham as our father — God can raise up children for Abraham from these stones. The axe is already laid at the root of the trees. Every tree that doesn't produce good fruit will be cut down and thrown into the fire. Lineage won't save anyone. John dismantles religious complacency before Jesus even arrives.

John declares: I baptise you in water for repentance, but the one coming after me is mightier than I — I'm not worthy to carry his sandals. He will baptise you in the Holy Spirit and with fire. His winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will thoroughly cleanse his threshing floor. He will gather his wheat into the barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire. John frames the coming Messiah as both saviour and judge. The same wind that gathers the wheat scatters the chaff.

Jesus comes from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptised by John. John tries to prevent him: I need to be baptised by you, and you come to me? Jesus answers: allow it now, for this is the fitting way for us to fulfil all righteousness. John consents. When Jesus comes up from the water, the heavens open, and he sees the Spirit of God descending like a dove and landing on him. A voice from heaven says: this is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased. The Trinity is on full display — Father speaking, Spirit descending, Son rising from the water. Jesus doesn't need cleansing; he's identifying with the people he came to save.
